title: How to win in the Aviator Game?
summary: Read the latest Gaming update on our blog.
content:
The Aviator game has become one of the most popular online games among players who enjoy simple gameplay and exciting rewards. Unlike traditional casino games, Aviator offers a unique experience where players must decide the perfect moment to cash out before the plane flies away. While the game is based on chance, there are several strategies that can help players make smarter decisions and improve their overall experience.

Many players on the BG678 Game platform enjoy Aviator because of its easy-to-understand rules, fast rounds, and engaging gameplay. Learning how the game works and managing your bets carefully can make a big difference over time.


  
  
  Understanding How Aviator Works


Before trying to win in Aviator, it is important to understand the basic rules. In each round, a plane takes off and a multiplier starts increasing from 1.00x. The longer the plane stays in the air, the higher the multiplier becomes.

Players place their bets before the round begins. During the flight, they must choose when to cash out. If they cash out before the plane flies away, they win their bet multiplied by the displayed multiplier. However, if the plane disappears before they cash out, they lose their stake.

The main challenge of Aviator is deciding the right time to take your winnings. Waiting longer can increase profits, but it also increases the risk of losing everything for that round.


  
  
  Start with Small Bets


One of the most important tips for beginners is to start with small bets. Many new players become excited after seeing high multipliers and place large bets too quickly.

Starting with smaller amounts allows you to:


Learn how the game behaves.
Understand the risk involved.
Protect your balance from large losses.
Gain confidence before increasing your stake.


Players on BG 678 Game often find that careful bankroll management is more effective than chasing quick wins.


  
  
  Set a Budget Before Playing


A successful player always knows how much money they are willing to spend before starting a session. Setting a budget helps prevent emotional decisions and keeps the experience enjoyable.

A good budget plan includes:


A maximum amount you can afford to lose.
A target profit amount.
A clear stopping point.


Once you reach your limit, it is best to stop playing. This approach helps maintain control and prevents unnecessary losses.


  
  
  Use Auto Cash Out Wisely


Many Aviator platforms offer an Auto Cash Out feature. This tool automatically cashes out your bet when a selected multiplier is reached.

For example:


Auto Cash Out at 1.50x provides safer and more frequent wins.
Auto Cash Out at 2.00x offers balanced risk and reward.
Auto Cash Out at 5.00x or higher carries much greater risk.


Using Auto Cash Out can remove emotional decision-making and help players stick to their chosen strategy.


  
  
  Avoid Chasing Losses


One of the biggest mistakes players make is trying to recover losses immediately. After losing several rounds, some players increase their bets dramatically in the hope of winning everything back.

This approach is risky because:
Losses can continue.
Your balance may disappear quickly.
Emotional decisions often lead to poor choices.

Instead, stay calm and continue following your planned betting strategy. Successful Aviator players focus on consistency rather than trying to recover losses in a single round.


  
  
  Observe Game Patterns Carefully


Although Aviator outcomes are random, many players like to observe previous rounds before placing bets. Looking at recent multipliers can help you understand how the game is progressing.

While past results do not predict future outcomes, observing trends may help you feel more comfortable with your betting decisions.

Remember that every round is independent. There is no guaranteed pattern that ensures a win.


  
  
  Use the Dual Bet Feature


Many versions of Aviator allow players to place two bets in the same round. This feature can be useful for balancing risk.

For example:
First bet cashes out automatically at 1.50x.
Second bet remains active for a higher multiplier.

This strategy provides a safer option while still giving players a chance to benefit from larger multipliers.

Many experienced users on BG678 Game use dual betting to create a balance between steady returns and higher-risk opportunities.


  
  
  Know When to Take Profits


Winning is not only about making successful bets. It is also about knowing when to stop.

Some players continue playing after achieving a good profit and eventually lose their winnings. Setting a profit target can help avoid this situation.

For example:


Aim for a 20% profit increase.
Stop when your target is reached.
Return another day instead of risking all your gains.


Discipline is often more important than any betting strategy.


  
  
  Stay Focused While Playing


Aviator is a fast-paced game with rounds that can end quickly. Staying focused helps players make better decisions.

To improve concentration:


Play in a quiet environment.
Avoid distractions.
Take regular breaks.
Do not play when tired or stressed.


Clear thinking can help you follow your strategy more effectively.


  
  
  Manage Your Bankroll Properly


Bankroll management is one of the most important skills for long-term success. Even the best strategies cannot eliminate risk completely.

A simple bankroll plan includes:


Divide your balance into smaller betting units.
Never risk a large percentage on one round.
Increase bets gradually when appropriate.
Reduce bet sizes after losses.


Proper bankroll management helps players stay in the game longer and reduces the impact of losing streaks.


  
  
  Learn from Your Experience


Every gaming session provides valuable lessons. Keeping track of your results can help you identify what works best for you.

Consider noting:


Your starting balance.
Bet sizes used.
Cash out points.
Overall results.


Over time, these records can help you develop a more effective personal strategy.


  
  
  Common Mistakes to Avoid


Many players struggle because they make avoidable mistakes. Some of the most common include:


Betting too much too soon.
Ignoring a budget.
Chasing losses.
Waiting too long to cash out.
Playing emotionally.
Expecting guaranteed wins.


Avoiding these mistakes can improve your overall experience and help you play more responsibly.


  
  
  Conclusion


Aviator is an exciting game that combines quick decision-making with simple gameplay mechanics. While no strategy can guarantee a win because the game is based on random outcomes, players can improve their chances by using smart betting habits, managing their bankroll carefully, and staying disciplined.

Whether you are a beginner or an experienced player, focusing on consistency, patience, and responsible gaming is the best approach. Many players on BG678 Game enjoy Aviator because it offers entertainment, fast action, and the opportunity to test different strategies. By understanding the game, setting clear limits, and making thoughtful decisions, you can enjoy a more rewarding Aviator gaming experience.

title: U4GM: The Ultimate Forza Horizon 6 Auction House Selling Guide
summary: Read the latest Gaming update on our blog.
content:
There's a funny moment in Forza Horizon 6 when you open the garage and realise half the cars in there haven't moved an inch. You won one from a spin, picked up another from an event, then grabbed a duplicate without even noticing. It happens fast. The Auction House is the cleanest way to turn that dead weight into useful money, especially when you're trying to build up FH6 Credits for upgrades, rare cars, or the next thing you've got your eye on.

Check the market before you list

Don't throw a car up for sale blind. Take a minute and search the same model first. Look at what people are asking, but more importantly, look at what actually seems to move. Some sellers price cars like they're made of gold, then the listing just sits there. If a car is tied to a recent seasonal reward, its value might dip because loads of players have it. Wait a week or two and demand can change. That little bit of patience can mean a much better sale.

Use a low starting bid to pull people in

A high starting price feels safe, but it can scare buyers away. Most players browsing auctions are hunting for a deal, even if they're willing to spend more once bidding starts. A low opening bid makes the listing look active and approachable. Once a few people jump in, they often keep fighting over it. That's where the price climbs. It's not guaranteed, of course, but it usually gives your car a better chance than sitting there with a stiff opening number nobody wants to touch.

Set the buyout with confidence

The buyout price is where rare cars can really pay off. If the vehicle is hard to get, limited, or just popular in the community, don't be shy about pushing the buyout toward the upper limit the game allows. Some buyers don't want to wait. They want the car now, tuned later, photographed tonight, done. That kind of player will often pay extra to skip the bidding war. For common cars, stay realistic. For rare reward cars, give yourself room to make a proper profit.

Time your auctions properly

Short auctions can work if you're flipping cheap cars quickly, but they're not ideal for anything valuable. A longer auction gives more players time to see it, especially across different time zones. Listing during busy hours helps too. Evenings and weekends tend to bring more eyes to the Auction House. If you've got a desirable car, don't rush it out at a quiet time and hope for the best. Treat the timing like part of the sale, because it really is.

Keep selling as your garage grows

The Auction House works best when you use it regularly, not only when your garage is packed. Clear duplicates, watch for cars that have risen in demand, and don't get too attached to vehicles you never drive. A steady habit of selling can keep your balance healthy without making the game feel like a job. If you're saving for something expensive, extra Forza Horizon 6 Credits from smart auctions can make the grind feel much lighter while still leaving you with a garage you actually enjoy using.

title: Golang Development in 2026: Benefits, Features & Business Applications | CodeChain Technologies
summary: Read the latest Dev update on our blog.
content:
Golang Development has become one of the fastest-growing areas in modern software engineering. Created by Google, Golang, also known as Go, is a powerful programming language designed for performance, simplicity, and scalability. In 2026, businesses are increasingly adopting Golang to build high-performance web applications, cloud-native platforms, APIs, microservices, and enterprise software.

As digital transformation continues across industries, organizations require technologies that can handle large-scale workloads, support cloud environments, and deliver exceptional speed. Golang addresses these challenges by offering a lightweight architecture, efficient concurrency, and easy maintenance. Its growing popularity among startups, enterprises, and cloud service providers makes it one of the most valuable backend technologies today.

CodeChain Technologies helps businesses leverage Golang development to create reliable, scalable, and future-ready applications that support long-term growth and innovation.


  
  
  What is Golang?


Golang is an open-source programming language for web development by Google to simplify software development while maintaining high performance.

It was designed to solve common challenges in modern application development, including:


Scalability
Concurrency
Performance optimization
Code simplicity
Cloud deployment


Go combines the speed of compiled languages with a developer-friendly syntax, making it ideal for modern software projects.


  
  
  Why Golang is Popular in 2026


1. High Performance

One of Golang's biggest advantages is its speed.

Benefits include:


Fast execution
Quick compilation
Low memory usage
Efficient processing


This makes Golang an excellent choice for performance-critical applications.

2. Excellent Scalability

Modern applications often need to support thousands or even millions of users.

Golang provides:


Efficient resource management
Horizontal scalability
Cloud-native compatibility
High system reliability
Businesses can scale applications without sacrificing performance.


3. Built-In Concurrency

Concurrency allows web applications to perform multiple tasks simultaneously.

Golang uses Goroutines to simplify concurrent programming.

Advantages include:


Faster processing
Better resource utilization
Improved application responsiveness


This feature is especially useful for APIs, real-time systems, and cloud services.

4. Simple and Clean Syntax

Golang is known for its straightforward and readable code.

Benefits include:


Easier maintenance
Faster onboarding
Reduced development complexity
Improved team productivity


Developers can build and maintain applications more efficiently.


  
  
  Key Applications of Golang Development


Web Application Development

Golang is widely used for building modern web applications.

Benefits include:


Fast response times
Strong security
High scalability
Efficient backend processing


Many organizations use Go for business-critical web platforms.

API Development

Golang is one of the best languages for API development.

Its lightweight architecture allows developers to create:


REST APIs
GraphQL APIs

Cloud service integrations
Enterprise APIs


High-speed performance makes Go ideal for API-driven systems.

Cloud-Native Applications

Cloud computing continues to drive technology innovation in 2026.

Golang is commonly used for:


Cloud platforms
Containerized applications
Distributed systems
Infrastructure tools


Many popular cloud technologies have been built using Go.

Microservices Architecture

Microservices help businesses create flexible and scalable software systems.

Golang supports:


Independent services
Faster deployments
Easier maintenance
Improved scalability


This makes it a preferred language for modern software architectures.

Enterprise Software

Large organizations use Golang to build enterprise-grade solutions.

Common applications include:


Business management systems
Financial software
Data processing platforms
Internal enterprise tools


Its reliability makes it suitable for mission-critical applications.


  
  
  Advantages of Golang Development


Faster Development
Simple syntax allows teams to build applications more efficiently.

Reduced Infrastructure Costs
Efficient resource usage helps lower server expenses.

Strong Security
Built-in features help improve application security.

Easy Deployment
Compiled binaries simplify deployment across different environments.

Large Community Support
Golang has a rapidly growing developer ecosystem and extensive documentation.


  
  
  Golang Development Trends in 2026


Cloud-First Development
Businesses continue adopting cloud-native solutions powered by Go.

DevOps and Automation
Many automation and infrastructure tools rely on Golang.

Microservices Growth
Organizations are increasingly moving toward microservices architectures.

Real-Time Applications
Go is being used for chat systems, streaming platforms, and live data processing.

Enterprise Modernization
Businesses are replacing legacy systems with scalable Golang-based solutions.


  
  
  Why Businesses Choose Golang


Organizations prefer Golang because it offers:


Exceptional performance
High scalability
Faster development cycles
Lower operational costs
Cloud-native compatibility
Long-term maintainability


These benefits help businesses build future-ready software solutions.


  
  
  Conclusion


Golang Development continues to gain momentum in 2026 as businesses seek high-performance, scalable, and cloud-ready technologies. Its combination of speed, simplicity, concurrency, and reliability makes it one of the best programming languages for modern backend development.

Whether building APIs, microservices, enterprise software, or cloud-native applications, Golang provides the tools needed to create secure and efficient solutions. As organizations increasingly focus on digital transformation and scalability, Go remains a top choice for developers and businesses worldwide.

CodeChain Technologies specializes in Golang development services, helping businesses create robust, scalable, and future-ready applications that deliver exceptional performance and long-term value.


  
  
  FAQs


1. What is Golang used for?
Golang is used for web development, API development, cloud applications, microservices, enterprise software, and automation tools.

2. Why is Golang popular in 2026?
Golang is popular because of its high performance, scalability, concurrency support, and cloud-native capabilities.

3. Is Golang good for web development?
Yes, Golang is excellent for building fast, secure, and scalable web applications and backend systems.

4. What are the advantages of Golang?
Key advantages include speed, low memory usage, simple syntax, concurrency support, and easy deployment.

5. Is Golang suitable for enterprise applications?
Yes, many enterprises use Golang for mission-critical systems, cloud platforms, APIs, and large-scale software solutions.

title: How AI Is Changing SEO: What Agencies Won't Tell You (But Should)
summary: Read the latest Dev update on our blog.
content:
I have been implementing and managing SEO projects for over fifteen years. I have watched Google roll out Panda and wipe out content farms overnight. I watched Penguin tear through link networks and link building processes that agencies had spent years building for their clients. I watched the Helpful Content Update quietly demote entire sites that had been producing volume over value for years.

Every time, the industry went through the same cycle: panic, denial, adaptation, and then a new set of people claiming they had cracked the code.

The impact of AI is different for many reasons, not least that it is more dramatic than those updates, but also because it is not an update at all. It is a structural change to what search actually is and a tool for how businesses operate. And most agencies, including mine for a while, have been slow to say that out loud.

This article is my honest account of how AI is changing SEO, why I resisted it longer than I should have, what changed my mind, and what it actually means for anyone trying to rank a website or decide whether to pay for SEO in 2026.


  
  
  What SEO Involves (Before We Talk About AI)



  
  
  The Six Pillars of a Real SEO Campaign


Before I get into how AI is changing SEO, I want to be clear about what SEO actually is, because a lot of the conversation around AI and search assumes everyone is just brainstorming or writing blog posts. Real SEO is considerably more involved than that.

A properly run SEO campaign covers at least six distinct areas. Each one requires expertise, time, and consistent execution. Every single one is now being transformed by AI.




Pillar
What it involves
How AI is changing it




Technical Audit
Crawling for broken links, duplicate content, redirect chains, slow pages, rendering issues
AI agents can now interpret crawl data, rank issues by impact, and draft fixes, cutting days of specialist work to hours


Keyword & Competitor Research
Identifying search intent, gap analysis, competitor benchmarking
Semantic clustering, intent mapping, and gap analysis across thousands of variants now done in a fraction of the time


Technical SEO
Schema markup, canonical tags, XML sitemaps, hreflang, site architecture
Schema, meta tags, and sitemaps can be generated, validated, and maintained by AI agents on custom workflows


On-Site Optimisation
Title tags, meta descriptions, heading structure, internal linking, image alt text
AI can audit and generate on-page elements at scale, with human review at the quality checkpoint


Content Creation
Researched, optimised editorial copy (on a busy month, over 100,000 words for our clients alone)
AI-assisted production shifts the role from writing to prompting, quality checking, and strategic direction


Link Building
Earning backlinks from authoritative sources, still one of Google's strongest trust signals
AI assists with prospecting and outreach drafts; human relationships and editorial judgement remain essential




That is what you are paying for when you hire an SEO agency.




  
  
  How AI Is Changing SEO Across Every One of Those Pillars



  
  
  Automated Audits and Technical SEO


Technical SEO was always the most automatable part of the job, even before AI. Tools like Screaming Frog, Website Auditor and SurferSEO have been crawling sites and flagging issues for years. What AI changes is the interpretation layer.

A crawler can tell you that a page has a slow server response time or broken links. An AI agent, fine-tuned with the right prompting and trained on your site's specific architecture, can tell you why, rank the issues by likely impact, draft the fix, and push it to a staging environment for review.

Schema markup, meta tags, sitemaps, canonical tags - all of this can now be generated, validated, and maintained by agents running on custom workflows. The technical SEO work that used to take a specialist several days per site can now be handled in hours, with a human reviewing the output rather than producing it from scratch.


  
  
  Keyword and Competitor Research at Machine Speed


Keyword research used to involve pulling data from multiple tools, building spreadsheets, identifying clusters, mapping intent, and then cross-referencing against what competitors were ranking for. A thorough research phase for a new client could take the best part of a week.

AI-assisted research changes the quality ceiling. Pattern recognition across thousands of keyword variants, semantic clustering, intent mapping, and gap analysis against the top ten ranking pages. All of this can now be done in a fraction of the time, and with a level of cross-referencing that would be impractical to do manually. The output still needs a strategist to interpret it and make decisions, but the raw analytical work is no longer the bottleneck it was.


  
  
  Content Creation: The Truth About AI vs. Human Copywriters


This is where it gets uncomfortable, and where I think most agencies are not being straight with their clients.

A skilled SEO copywriter producing a well-researched, properly optimised 4,000-word article takes between one and three days. Factor in the briefing, the research, the draft, the review, the revisions, and the final quality check, and you are looking at a meaningful cost per piece. For a client on a content-heavy SEO programme, those costs add up quickly.

AI can produce a comparable piece for pennies. The human time involved shifts from writing to prompting, quality checking, providing feedback, and iterating. A professional SEO strategist spending two to three hours on a piece, including the research brief, the AI generation, the quality review against tools like SurferSEO, and the final editorial pass, can produce something that matches or exceeds what a human copywriter would produce in three days.

The expert is still essential. The role has just changed entirely. You are no longer paying for typing. You are paying for the knowledge of what to write, how to structure it, what gaps to fill, what the competition is missing, and how to quality-check the output so it does not end up as the generic, hallucination-riddled content that gives AI a bad name.

To put some numbers around this, here is how the three approaches compare using industry averages:




Approach
Time per 4,000-word article
Typical cost (UK agency rates)
Quality ceiling
Risk level




Human-only copywriter
2–3 days (inc. brief, draft, revisions)
£400–£900 per article
High, if experienced
Low, if well-briefed


AI-assisted (agency-led)
2–3 hours (inc. research brief, generation, QA)
£150–£350 per article
Higher, due to thorough research iteration
Low, with proper oversight


DIY ChatGPT (no process)
20–30 minutes
Near zero
Low: generic, untargeted output
High: can actively harm rankings






The cost saving in the AI SEO agency model is real and significant. But the saving comes from production efficiency, not from cutting the expertise. That distinction is what most businesses miss when they try to replicate the process themselves.


  
  
  Link Building and Outreach in an AI-First World


Link building is the area where I believe AI has made the least progress, and where human relationships and genuine editorial judgement still matter most. AI can help identify prospects, draft outreach emails, and analyse the authority of potential linking domains. AI agents can even automate content submission and publication processes online. But the actual relationship-building, the pitching of genuinely original ideas to editors, and the creation of content worth linking to -this all still requires human input.

That said, the content that earns links is increasingly the content that AI helps produce, because the speed and scale of production means you can create more genuinely useful resources in less time. 


  
  
  Why I Resisted AI SEO (And Later Changed My Mind)



  
  
  The Penguin and Panda Lessons & Reason for Caution


If you have been doing SEO long enough to remember the pre-Panda world, you will understand why my default position on AI content was scepticism. In the early days, you could rank almost anything with enough keyword repetition and enough links.

Then Panda arrived in 2011 and wiped out content farms. Penguin followed in 2012 and destroyed link networks. Every time the industry found a shortcut, Google found a way to close it.

Automation has always been on Google's list of things to watch. Mass-produced content, spun articles, auto-generated pages, etc. These all have been targeted at one point or another. So when AI content tools started appearing, my instinct was the same as it had been every other time to assume it was a shortcut, and shortcuts get penalised.

For a long time, I held the line. My team produced human-written, individually optimised content (and still do). On a busy month, we were producing over 100,000 words of copy for clients, all of it written and reviewed by people. It was expensive, it was slow by comparison, but it was safe.


  
  
  Google's Official Stance on AI Content


Google's official guidance on AI-generated content, published in February 2023, is clear on the distinction that matters:


"Using automation (including AI) to generate content with the primary purpose of manipulating ranking in search results is a violation of our spam policies. This said, it's important to recognize that not all use of automation, including AI generation, is spam."

Source: Google Search Quality Team, February 2023


Their ranking systems are designed to reward original, high-quality content that demonstrates E-E-A-T: experience, expertise, authoritativeness, and trustworthiness. The method of production is not the issue. The quality and intent of the output is.

My position started to change when I could see that AI-generated content was clearly superior to human-written content with the right process behind it. Rather than relying on what the SEO community was saying about it, I decided to rely on what I could see with my own eyes and gradually started to test whether AI content would be valued and ranked by Google. 

The Helpful Content system targets content that is produced primarily for search engines rather than for people. Google is direct about what this means in practice:


"People-first content means content that's created primarily for people, and not to manipulate search engine rankings. Does your content clearly demonstrate first-hand expertise and a depth of knowledge? After reading your content, will someone leave feeling they've learned enough about a topic to help achieve their goal?"

Source: Google, Creating helpful, reliable, people-first content


The system does not care whether a human or a machine wrote the content. A generic, low-value article written by a human is just as likely to be demoted as a generic, low-value article written by AI. What Google rewards is content that demonstrates genuine expertise, provides original insight, and gives the reader something they could not get from the first ten results they already looked at.

That matters enormously, because the question is not "should I use AI for SEO?" It is "am I using AI to produce something genuinely better, or am I using it to produce something faster and cheaper at the expense of quality?"




  
  
  The Moment the Tide Turned


The thing that finally changed my mind was watching what was happening with my own websites and seeing what our clients were doing themselves, without even speaking to us.

We had published a blog post on AI and the future of SEO, written partially by AI and humans, deeply researched, and produced the way we had always produced content. Within a month or two, it was generating over 76,000 impressions and sitting at an average position of 8.7 across sixteen different search queries.

The top queries were not the ones we had explicitly optimised for. They were variations: the future of seo with ai, how has seo changed with ai, will seo be replaced by ai, is seo dead with ai. The article was ranking for the full semantic cluster around the topic, not just the primary keyword.

That told me something important: Google was not rewarding keyword targeting. It was rewarding genuine topical depth and first-hand expertise.

In parallel, we started testing AI-assisted content on our own sites with proper process, proper prompting, and proper quality control. It worked. The results were comparable to our human-written content, and in some cases better, because the research and iteration phases were more thorough than we could afford to be manually.

We went even further - our latest site is a complete agentic build, using Antigravity and Claude Opus to construct the entire Astro site, with many of the pages AI-generated from the ground up. Those pages are now ranking alongside the human-written ones because the process behind them was rigorous enough that the output met the same quality standard.

At the same time, something else was happening with our clients. Some of them had started using ChatGPT to produce content in parallel with our own work. We would notice it when the content on their site started to drift in quality, e.g. generic phrasing, hallucinated statistics, topics covered at a surface level without any of the depth or specificity that makes content useful. In some cases, their AI-generated content was clearly impacting the overall quality signal of their site, diluting the work we had done.

Sadly, some of those clients cancelled their contracts with us, but most likely because they had decided that ChatGPT was good enough and considerably cheaper. Although they never said that directly, it was always things like "we're reviewing our marketing spend" or "we're going to handle content internally for a while." 

At this point, I decided the market had already decided that AI was part of the picture, and our own evidence was strong enough for us to fully embrace AI as an agency. The question was whether other agencies were going to follow suit or insist on a purely human process while clients went and did it badly on their own.


  
  
  Should I Pay for SEO in 2026?


This is the question I get asked more than any other right now, and it deserves a straight answer. Google's own guidance on E-E-A-T is relevant here, because it explains exactly what is and is not worth paying for:


"Trust is the most important component of E-E-A-T. The others contribute to trust, but content doesn't necessarily have to demonstrate all of them. Some content might be helpful based on the experience it demonstrates, while other content might be helpful because of the expertise it shares."

Source: Google, E-E-A-T and the Quality Rater Guidelines, December 2022


The implication for SEO spend is direct: you are not paying for content production. You are paying for demonstrated expertise and the trust signals that come with it.




  
  
  What You Are Actually Paying For Has Changed


The value in a professional SEO service has never really been the writing. It has been the strategy, the research, the technical knowledge, and the quality control. AI has made the production side of SEO faster and cheaper. It has not made the expertise side any less important.

If you use ChatGPT to write an article without doing proper keyword research first, you will produce something that covers the topic at a generic level, misses the specific search intent behind the queries you want to rank for, and looks identical to the hundreds of other AI-generated articles on the same topic, much like an article that has been spun very well. For UK British content, it will probably be written using US spellings and using all of those common terms that are clearly AI-generated, like "Realm", "Delve", "Robust", "Comprehensive", etc. Not that there is anything wrong with these words, but they are a clear giveaway. For all these reasons and more, chances are, it won't rank.

If you use AI with a proper research brief, a clear understanding of what the top-ranking pages are missing, a structured prompt that incorporates your specific expertise and perspective, and a quality review against SEO best practice, you will produce something that can rank and that provides genuine value to the reader.

The difference between those two outcomes is expertise. That is what you are paying for.


  
  
  The Risk of Doing It Yourself With ChatGPT


The most common mistake I see businesses make is treating AI as a content vending machine. They put in a topic, they get out an article, they publish it, and they wonder why nothing happens.

The problems are predictable and consistent:



No keyword gap analysis: the article targets phrases that are either too competitive or have no search volume.

No competitor benchmarking: the article covers the same ground as everything already ranking, with nothing new to offer.

No schema markup: the page is invisible to AI-powered search features and knowledge panels.

No internal linking strategy: the page sits in isolation rather than contributing to the site's topical authority.

No quality check: hallucinated facts, inconsistent tone, and the generic phrasing that Google's systems are increasingly good at identifying as low-value.


Doing SEO badly with AI is not cheaper than doing it well with an agency. It is just a different way of wasting money, with the added risk of actively damaging your site's standing with Google.


  
  
  What a Real AI SEO Agency Does Differently


What my agency now offers, alongside our traditional human-led services, is an AI-driven SEO service that provides an alternative, built around expertise rather than volume. The process starts with detailed keyword and competitor research, identifying not just what to write about but what angle to take, what the existing content is missing, and what a genuinely better piece would look like. The AI is then prompted and trained on specific rules, processes, and quality standards, not just asked to write something. The output is reviewed against SEO best practice, checked for accuracy, and revised until it meets the standard we would apply to human-written copy.

The cost saving is real, and the quality ceiling is higher than it was with purely human production, because the research and iteration phases are more thorough. But the expertise requirement has not gone away. If anything, it has increased, because the gap between a well-run AI SEO process and a poorly run one is larger than the gap between a good human copywriter and a bad one.


  
  
  What Google and AI Search Engines Now Expect



  
  
  Generative Engine Optimisation (GEO): Being Cited by ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity


Search is a growing proportion of information-seeking behaviour that now happens through ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, and other AI-powered tools that synthesise answers rather than returning a list of links. Being visible in those answers requires a different kind of optimisation. Research published by BrightEdge in 2025 found that AI-powered search features were appearing in over 84% of search queries, fundamentally changing the relationship between content and visibility.


"The shift from ranking to retrieval is the defining change in search. The question is no longer just 'can Google find my page?' It is 'will an AI system select my content as the best available answer?'"

Source: BrightEdge Research, AI Search Trends Report, 2025


Generative Engine Optimisation, or GEO, is about structuring your content so that AI systems select it as a source when generating responses. This means writing in clear, declarative sentences that are easy to extract. It means covering topics with enough depth and specificity that the model can use your content to answer follow-up questions, not just the headline query. It means establishing your brand as a trustworthy entity through consistent, accurate information across multiple sources.

The overlap with traditional SEO is significant. Content that is well-structured, authoritative, and genuinely useful tends to perform well in both traditional search and generative AI responses. But there are specific technical steps that help like clear heading hierarchies, FAQ sections that mirror the conversational queries people ask AI tools, and structured data that helps models understand what your business does and who it serves.




  
  
  How Traditional SEO Compares to AI-First SEO


The shift is not just about tools. The entire optimisation model has changed. Here is how the two approaches differ across the signals that matter:




Factor
Traditional SEO (pre-2023)
AI-First SEO (2024 onwards)




Primary goal
Rank on page 1 of Google
Be cited in AI Overviews, featured snippets, and generative answers


Success metric
Organic clicks and keyword rankings
Impressions, AI citations, brand mentions, and zero-click visibility


Content format
Keyword-optimised long-form articles
Declarative, question-answering content structured for extraction


Key ranking signals
Backlinks, keyword density, page authority
E-E-A-T, entity clarity, topical depth, structured data


Technical priority
Crawlability, page speed, mobile-first
All of the above, plus schema, llms.txt, and entity disambiguation


Link building
Volume of backlinks from authoritative domains
Same, but content must be genuinely citation-worthy to earn links


Tooling
Screaming Frog, Ahrefs, SEMrush, manual copywriting
AI agents, semantic analysis tools, GEO monitoring, LLM visibility tracking







  
  
  Answer Engine Optimisation (AEO): Designing for Zero-Click and Voice Search


A substantial proportion of Google searches now end without a click. The user gets their answer from the AI Overview, the featured snippet, or the knowledge panel, and they never visit a website. For informational queries, this proportion is even higher. According to data from SparkToro and Datos, approximately 60% of Google searches in 2024 ended without a click to any website, a figure that has only increased with the rollout of AI Overviews.

Answer Engine Optimisation is about remaining visible in that zero-click environment. It means structuring content to answer specific questions directly, in the first paragraph of a section rather than buried three hundred words in. It means using the exact phrasing that people use when they ask questions, because AI systems match on natural language rather than keyword density. It means building content that is genuinely the best available answer to a specific question, because that is what gets surfaced.

For agencies and businesses, this changes the metrics that matter. Impressions and AI citations become as important as clicks. Brand visibility in AI-generated answers is a form of reach that does not show up in traditional analytics but absolutely influences purchase decisions.


  
  
  Large Language Model Optimisation (LLMO): Becoming Part of What the Model Knows


Beyond individual search queries, there is a longer-term question about whether your brand exists in the knowledge base of the major AI models. When someone asks ChatGPT to recommend an SEO agency in Birmingham, does your agency come up? When someone asks Gemini about the future of SEO, does your content get cited?

This is what Large Language Model Optimisation addresses. It involves ensuring your brand has clear, consistent, machine-readable information available across the web: structured data with sameAs links connecting your profiles across LinkedIn, Google Business Profile, Crunchbase, and other authoritative sources; an llms.txt file that gives AI crawlers a clear, concise summary of what your business does and what your content covers; and entity-level clarity so that models can distinguish you from other businesses with similar names or services.

None of this replaces the fundamentals of SEO. It builds on them. If Google cannot crawl your site, no AI system will cite it either.




  
  
  Technical SEO Is the Foundation: AI Visibility Has Nowhere to Anchor Without It


This is the part that gets lost in conversations about GEO and AEO and the future of search. All of the new optimisation strategies depend on the same foundation that traditional SEO has always depended on: a site that is properly crawlable, properly indexed, and properly structured.

If your pages are slow to load, if your JavaScript rendering is blocking Googlebot from seeing your content, if your internal linking structure is a mess, if your schema is missing or incorrect, none of the content work matters. AI crawlers use the same infrastructure as traditional search crawlers. They cannot cite what they cannot read.

Internal linking is particularly important in an AI-first world. The way LLMs map topical authority is partly through the connections between pages. A site where every page links logically to related content, where the architecture reflects genuine expertise across a topic area, signals authority in a way that isolated pages cannot. This is not new thinking in SEO, but it is more important now than it has ever been.


  
  
  AI SEO Services: What the New Model Actually Looks Like



  
  
  Pure AI-Driven SEO vs. Hybrid Human/AI


At Opace, we now offer both. The choice depends on budget, risk tolerance, and the specific goals of the campaign. Here is how the two models compare in practice:





Hybrid Human/AI
Pure AI-Driven




Who handles strategy
Human SEO strategist
Human at key checkpoints only


Who handles production
AI, with human QA and editorial pass
AI agents throughout


Best for
Most businesses; balances quality and cost
High-volume content needs with strong internal oversight


Content volume
Moderate to high
High


Cost
Mid-range
Lower


Risk
Low, with proper process
Medium: quality depends entirely on process quality


Suitable if you want to
Outsource the whole process
Maintain internal control with agency setup and training




What neither model involves is asking ChatGPT to write articles and publishing whatever comes out. That is not AI SEO. That is the thing that gives AI SEO a bad name.




  
  
  What Makes an AI SEO Agency Worth Paying For


The expertise in an AI SEO service is not in the tools. It is in the process. Any business can access ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude. What they cannot easily replicate is the accumulated knowledge of what makes content rank, the prompting frameworks built from years of SEO practice, the quality standards that distinguish useful content from generic content, and the technical implementation that ensures the content is properly structured, properly linked, and properly indexed.

When you work with a professional SEO agency, you are paying for the expertise to use these tools in a way that produces results, not just output. The distinction matters more now than it ever did, because the gap between well-executed AI SEO and poorly executed AI SEO is larger than the gap between good and bad traditional SEO.


  
  
  An AI SEO Audit Checklist: Where to Start


If you want to assess where your site stands against the new requirements of AI-first search, here is a practical starting point. This is not a complete list, but it covers the areas that make the biggest difference.

Technical foundation:


Is every important page crawlable and indexable? Check your robots.txt and meta robots tags.
Are your Core Web Vitals in good shape? Slow pages lose visibility in both traditional and AI-powered search.
Is your JavaScript rendering correctly? If your content is loaded client-side, check that Googlebot can see it.
Do you have an XML sitemap that is up to date and submitted to Google Search Console?


Entity and structured data:


Do you have Organisation schema on your homepage with accurate name, url, logo, and sameAs properties?
Are your key service pages marked up with Service or Product schema?
Do you have an FAQ schema on pages that answer specific questions?
Is your llms.txt file in place, giving AI crawlers a clear summary of your site's purpose and content?


Content quality:


Does each page target a specific search intent, not just a keyword?
Does the content provide something that the top ten ranking pages do not? Original data, first-hand experience, a specific angle, a more complete answer?
Are your headings structured logically, with H2s covering the main topic areas and H3s covering specific questions within them?
Is the content written for people first? Would a reader find it genuinely useful, or does it read as though it was produced to satisfy a search engine?


Internal linking and topical authority:


Does your site have a clear topic cluster structure, with pillar pages covering broad topics and supporting pages covering specific subtopics?
Does every important page receive internal links from relevant pages elsewhere on the site?
Are your internal link anchor texts descriptive and keyword-relevant?


GEO and AEO readiness:


Are your key pages structured to answer specific questions directly, with the answer in the first paragraph rather than buried in the body?
Do you have FAQ sections that mirror the conversational queries people use in AI tools?
Is your brand mentioned consistently and accurately across external sources: Google Business Profile, LinkedIn, industry directories?





  
  
  Final Thoughts: The Agencies That Adapt Will Win


I have spent the last fifteen years watching the SEO industry change. Every major shift, from the link-building era to the content marketing era to the technical SEO era, has been accompanied by the same prediction: SEO is dead. It never was. It just changed.

This shift is bigger than the previous ones, because it is not just about how Google ranks pages. It is about what search is. The move from a list of links to a synthesised AI answer changes the relationship between content and visibility in a way that keyword optimisation alone cannot address. You can read more about how this evolution has unfolded in our analysis of AI and the future of SEO, which covers the technical mechanics of how LLMs differ from traditional search engines and what that means for long-term strategy.

The agencies that will do well are the ones that are honest with their clients about what has changed, that build the expertise to use AI tools properly rather than just cheaply, and that combine technical knowledge with genuine strategic thinking. The ones that will struggle are the ones that either pretend nothing has changed or hand everything to a language model without the oversight and expertise to make the output worth publishing.

My agency took longer than I would have liked to make that shift. But we made it. The work we are producing now, with the right combination of AI tools, human expertise, and proper process, is better than what we were producing before.

That is the honest answer to the question of how AI is changing SEO. It is not killing it. It is raising the bar for what good looks like.

The conversation about what SEO should look like in 2026, whether AI-driven, hybrid, or traditional, always starts in the same place - being clear about what you are trying to achieve and what the right process is to get there. That has always been true; it is just more important now.

About me: 
I am the founder of Opace Digital Agency, a Birmingham-based SEO and web design agency working with clients across the UK and internationally. I've been working in search engine optimisation since 2008 and publishing content on the evolving trends and changes taking place within the industry. Check out my profile on dev.to for more articles, or drop me a message 👇🏻 


    
      
        
      
    
  
    
David@OpaceFollow

    
      UK based web developer, open-source and AI enthusiast, and technology writer. Founder of https://opace.agency since 2008.

title: Cut Your Claude Token Consumption By 70x
summary: Read the latest AI update on our blog.
content:
A recent open-source tool can help you cut your token consumption by up to 70x!

The tool is called Graphify and is enjoying a well-deserved hype!




  
  
  What Graphify Does Differently


Graphify directly addresses token consumption in a simple and smart way.

You run it once.

It reads your entire project using an AST parser (tree-sitter, which supports 31 languages) and builds a knowledge graph that maps relationships, dependencies, and the most interconnected concepts in your code.

From that point forward, your AI agent doesn’t re-read files. It navigates the graph.

The benchmark from the author: 70x fewer tokens consumed per session!

All of that boils down to three output files:


graph.html — an interactive visualization you can open in any browser
GRAPH_REPORT.md — a readable summary of key concepts and surprising connections
graph.json — the full queryable graph for agents to use directly



  
  
  Three More Reasons To Use It


Obviously, the first and most important reason is the reduction of token consumption. But this is not the only one.


It’s not just for Claude Code. Cursor, Codex, OpenCode, Copilot CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot Chat, Aider — the list goes on. If your agent can read files, it can query the graph. The tool supports over 15 platforms out of the box.
Every relationship in the graph has a confidence tag. EXTRACTED — pulled directly from the code. INFERRED — logically deduced. AMBIGUOUS — uncertain. This is the kind of honesty most AI tools quietly skip. You always know what was found versus what was guessed. Then you can use Claude itself or other AIs to fix it.
It identifies “god nodes.” God nodes are the most connected concepts in your project, the cornerstones, or the pieces that everything else flows through. If you’ve just inherited a large codebase you didn’t write, god nodes are the first place you look, the busiest intersections. It’s the architectural map you never had time to draw.


On top of these reasons, the GitHub repo has over 53,000 stars.

That’s a positive, strong signal.

Once you try Graphify, you change the way you approach a codebase: the graph approach changes how you think about AI-assisted development.

Instead of dumping context, you query it.


  
  
  How to Install It


Requirements: Python 3.10+ and uv. That’s it.



uv tool install graphifyy
graphify install






Then open your AI assistant and type /graphify.

And that’s it.

MIT license. Free. Open source.

I hope your $20 plan will take you further!

title: How to Build an Investor Demo That Raises Funding
summary: Read the latest AI update on our blog.
content:
Most founders think their demo is a product tour. Investors think it's a proof of judgment. That gap is why technically brilliant teams walk out of rooms with nothing.

The demo isn't where you show off features. It's where you prove you understand the problem better than anyone else — and that you've already started solving it. Get that framing wrong, and the best product in the room still loses.

Here's how to build an investor demo that raises funding, based on what we've seen work across dozens of early-stage builds.


  
  
  The Demo Is a Sales Document, Not a Product Manual


Investors are not evaluating your UI. They're running a parallel mental calculation: Is this a real problem? Is this team the right one to solve it? Can this become a big business?

Every slide, screen, and prototype you show either advances that calculation or stalls it. The mistake most founders make is building a demo that answers "what does this do?" when investors are asking "why does this win?"

Your demo needs to answer three questions in under 10 minutes: What's broken in the market, how your solution fixes it uniquely, and what early evidence exists that you're right. Everything else is noise.


  
  
  What Investors Actually Look For


Traction signals carry more weight than any feature. Even $5K MRR, 200 waitlist signups, or a signed LOI from a recognizable brand tells investors more than a polished animation.

Narrative coherence matters as much as data. The story — problem, insight, solution, evidence — needs to move in a straight line. Investors sit through 10 demos a week. Confusion is a disqualifier.

Speed of comprehension is a hidden metric. If the investor needs to ask clarifying questions before they understand what you do, you've already lost momentum. The best demos are understood in 60 seconds and remembered in 60 days.


  
  
  Common Mistakes That Kill Funding Conversations


The most common mistake: leading with features. Founders open on a dashboard walkthrough before investors understand why the dashboard exists. You've made them work to find the story — and they won't.

The second mistake: demoing a half-built product with apologies. "This part isn't finished yet" is a phrase that kills confidence instantly. If a section isn't ready, don't show it. Build a focused prototype — a polished, clickable version of your core use case only — rather than exposing unfinished edges.

The third mistake: skipping the market frame entirely. A demo with no market sizing, no competitor awareness, and no customer voice looks like a side project, not a company.

One more: building the demo yourself, in the week before the meeting, while also running the company. The output quality shows — and investors notice.


  
  
  Real Example: Seed Round, 12-Person Team, 2 Weeks


One of our clients — a 12-person B2B SaaS company building ops tooling for logistics firms — came to us two weeks before a scheduled seed pitch. Their existing demo was a Loom walkthrough of a staging environment that lagged, showed test data, and ran 18 minutes long.

We rebuilt it from scratch: a live clickable prototype in Framer, a tight 8-slide narrative deck, and a 3-minute scripted demo flow that hit problem, solution, and one key traction metric — a signed pilot with a regional freight company.

They went into that meeting with something that looked fundable. The prototype showed the core workflow in four clicks. The deck had a market slide, a single competitor matrix, and a revenue model on one page. They raised a $600K pre-seed round six weeks later.

That's not magic. That's preparation compressing into signal.


  
  
  Tools That Build Demos Investors Remember


Framer: The fastest way to build high-fidelity interactive prototypes without engineering hours — used for polished click-through product demos.

Pitch: A collaborative deck tool with cleaner defaults than PowerPoint and real-time commenting — better for async investor review.

Loom: Still useful for async warm intros, but only for a tight 3-minute version, not a product walkthrough.

Rows: For embedding live data or financial models directly into decks — makes traction slides feel real and dynamic.

Notion: For building a supporting investor data room that you can link from the deck — diligence materials, team bios, customer references.

Descript: For cleaning up any recorded demo clips — removes filler, tightens pacing, looks professional in seconds.

The tool stack matters less than the discipline to cut. Every tool above can produce a bad demo if you put too much in it.


  
  
  How to Build an Investor Demo That Raises Funding: The Checklist




Nail the one-liner first. Write a single sentence that explains what you do, who it's for, and what changes for them. If you can't write it, you can't demo it.

Build a prototype, not a product tour. Show the core use case in 3–5 clicks. Anything beyond that is a distraction.

Open on the problem, not the product. Spend the first 90 seconds making the investor feel the pain you're solving — before you show a single screen.

Include one real customer voice. A quote, a case study stat, or a reference you can name. Investor confidence multiplies when someone outside the founding team has validated the problem.

Compress your deck to 10 slides maximum. Problem, solution, why now, market, traction, team, ask. Cut the rest.

Time yourself. Run the full demo in under 10 minutes. If you can't, you haven't cut enough.

Get an outside review before the room. Someone who doesn't know your product should watch your demo and tell you — without prompting — what you do and why it matters. If they can't, rebuild.


The founders who raise aren't always building the best products. They're building the clearest case that their product deserves to exist — and they're doing it before they walk in the room.




Originally published at showcase-it.com/blog




About ShowcaseIT

ShowcaseIT is a boutique AI strategy and automation studio helping startups and SMBs build investor demos, automate operations, and integrate AI into their business — in weeks, not months.


startup demo and product services
Book a free 15-minute call with Adam
Read more on the ShowcaseIT blog

title: Unlocking the Power of Agentic Workflows: The Next Frontier of Software Engineering
summary: How autonomous AI agents are moving from simple chatbots to complex multi-agent coding loops that write, test, and deploy entire microservices.
content:
For the past few years, developers have used large language models primarily as intelligent autocompletes or code synthesizers. However, a major paradigm shift is underway. The industry is moving from conversational prompts to agentic workflows.

In an agentic system, an AI model is wrapped in an execution loop. It can plan actions, invoke tools (like compilers, web searchers, and terminals), analyze the results, and iterate on its own code. When multiple specialized agents are set up to cooperate—such as a product manager agent, a developer agent, and a QA agent—they can build and deploy complete software applications autonomously.

This shift requires a new way of thinking about software design, testing frameworks, and system guardrails to ensure reliability and safety.

title: Next.js 15 & React 19: Demystifying the New Compiler Architecture
summary: An in-depth review of how React's new compiler automates memoization and optimizes component rendering under Next.js 15 architectures.
content:
React 19 and Next.js 15 represent one of the biggest leaps forward in frontend performance. The core highlight of this release is the React Compiler (React Forget), which analyzes code statically to automate rendering optimizations.

Historically, developers had to manually optimize component rendering using hooks like useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo. This was error-prone and added boilerplate. The new compiler automatically inserts these optimizations during the build step, ensuring that only the components that actually need to re-render are updated.

This article breaks down how the compilation step works, its impact on package bundle sizes, and how developers can prepare their codebases for transition.

title: The Rise of WebGPU: Bringing Desktop-Grade Graphics and AI Directly to the Web
summary: How the new WebGPU API is replacing WebGL to deliver high-performance 3D rendering and client-side machine learning execution in standard browsers.
content:
WebGL served the web well for over a decade, but it was designed around legacy graphics pipelines. WebGPU is the modern successor designed from the ground up to utilize modern GPU hardware APIs like Vulkan, Metal, and Direct3D 12.

By providing low-overhead access to GPU compute capabilities, WebGPU enables incredibly detailed 3D scenes to run at 60fps on standard desktop and mobile browsers. Beyond gaming and creative interfaces, WebGPU is a massive game-changer for AI. It allows large language models and diffusion networks to run client-side in the browser, eliminating server hosting costs and ensuring user privacy.

title: Moving Beyond Docker: Why Edge Computing is Embracing WebAssembly Containers
summary: Why infrastructure providers are migrating edge microservices from heavy Docker containers to ultra-lightweight, sub-millisecond Wasm environments.
content:
Docker containers revolutionized deployment, but their startup latencies and memory overheads make them poorly suited for modern edge networks. WebAssembly (Wasm) is filling this gap.

With startup times under 1 millisecond and footprints measured in kilobytes, Wasm containers allow hosting providers to run thousands of isolated microservices on a single edge server. Security is maintained through strict compile-time sandboxing, and developers can write their functions in any language (Rust, Go, C++, or TypeScript) and compile them to a single cross-platform Wasm binary. We explore the transition architectures adopted by major edge providers.

title: Preparing for Y2K38: Fixing the Unix Epoch Integer Overflow Before It's Too Late
summary: A technical look at the Year 2038 problem, why 32-bit systems will crash on January 19, 2038, and the systems engineering efforts to migrate global networks.
content:
Much like the Y2K scare of the late 90s, the Year 2038 problem (Y2K38) is a real time-bomb embedded in Unix-based operating systems. On January 19, 2038, the standard 32-bit signed integer representing seconds since January 1, 1970, will overflow and wrap around to a negative value.

This will cause systems to think the current time is December 13, 1901, leading to major crashes in database servers, network routers, and embedded devices. Systems engineers around the world are currently working to upgrade critical infrastructure to 64-bit time representations. We analyze the progress made, remaining legacy systems, and how to verify if your code is Y2K38 compliant.
